title: 26" Waterfall Framed End Table
summary: A compact single-shelf end table built with the same mitered waterfall corners as this catalog's larger console tables, scaled down to nightstand size.
description:
This is the same waterfall-miter technique as the catalog's larger console tables — 45° bevels on non-parallel ends so the top's line carries straight down the sides — shrunk to a 26"-tall end table built from a single wide project panel instead of narrower boards.

The source plan is upfront about the hard part: fitting all the mitered pieces onto one 12-foot project panel is genuinely tight, and it recommends a specific cutting order to make it work — cut one outside side panel first (with its one 45° bevel), then use that same bevel setting to cut the top, then flip the setup to cut the second side panel, and only then cut the remaining pieces from what's left. If you're newer to miters, buying two shorter panels (an 8-footer and a 6-footer) buys real margin for practice cuts and mistakes.

Make your practice cuts on scrap first and check them for square before touching the real panel — a bevel that's off by even half a degree is obvious once four of them have to meet at a corner.
timeLabel: 4–6 hrs, most of it in the miter cuts

tools.note: Pulls the 1x3 trim tight to the top and locks the miters.
Precision on the 45° bevels is what makes or breaks this build.

materials.name: Pine project panel, 16" wide, 12 ft
materials.note: Tight to fit everything on one panel — consider an 8 ft and a 6 ft panel instead if you're newer to miters.

cutList.note: Same width as your project panel.
Both ends beveled 45°, long point measurement, ends not parallel.
One end beveled 45°, long point measurement.
Long point to long point, both ends 45°, not parallel.
Long point to long point, both ends 45°, not parallel.

steps.title: Practice the bevel cuts, then cut the panels
steps.body: Make practice bevel cuts on scrap first and check them for square, adjusting your saw as needed — this is the hardest part of the whole build. Following the source's recommended order, cut one outside side panel first, then use that same saw setting to cut the top, then flip the setup to cut the second side panel, and cut the remaining pieces from what's left of the panel.

steps.title: Attach blocking and the top
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 inside cleats to the inside faces of the side panels with glue and brad nails. Attach the top to the sides at the mitered corners — if you have a pocket-hole jig, pocket-holing the 1x3 trim to the top here helps pull the miter tight.

steps.title: Add the bottom shelf
steps.body: Measure your actual 1x3 width before cutting the bottom shelf's position — it needs to sit so the distance from the top of the shelf to the bottom of the sides matches your 1x3 width exactly.

steps.title: Add the inside panels
steps.body: Cut the inside side panels to fit the gap left for your 1x3 trim width, then attach with glue and brad nails.

steps.title: Build and attach the mitered front and back frames
steps.body: Cut the front and back trim frames to fit, dry-fit everything before committing, then attach. Pocket-holing the miter corners together first makes them noticeably tighter. Nail and glue all the trim to the project with 1-1/4" brad nails.

images.alt: A small waterfall-mitered end table with a single open shelf
